attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for antenatal care. At 22 weeks and four days gestation, an obstetric ultrasound reveals a fetus with two vertical hypoechoic regions through the upper lip extending to the nostril on the right side. This defect causes distortion of the upper lip and nose, which is also seen on a 3D rendered image. No other anomalies are noted. The prenatal diagnosis is cleft lip and palate.

At 39 weeks and one day gestation, a live male newborn weighing 3.1 kg with facial abnormalities is delivered. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. An airway assessment shows patent bilateral nostrils with equal air entry in bilateral lung fields.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This newborn presents with cleft lip and palate, one of the most common congenital craniofacial abnormalities. The condition is characterised by the failure of normal fusion of the palate and lip at the midline during development, resulting in a clinically obvious deformity in the newborn.

The most appropriate next step in management is a feeding assessment. The initial evaluation of a newborn with cleft lip and palate should focus on respiratory and feeding issues to ensure the infant can grow and develop appropriately until definitive surgical repair. Newborns with cleft palates generally cannot generate sufficient negative intraoral pressure to suck milk effectively from a bottle or breast. However, they can be bottle-fed with formula or expressed breast milk using a feeding obturator.

Craniofacial unit consultation is indeed important, but it typically comes after the immediate feeding and respiratory concerns are addressed. Surgery is generally performed around three months for cleft lip and six months for cleft palate (corrected age if born prematurely), depending on weight.

Genetic consultation is advised due to the increased risk of clefts in future pregnancies, especially in syndromic cases. This allows for appropriate pre-conception measures and antenatal care in the future. However, it is not the most urgent step for the newborn's immediate care.

Audiology consultation is recommended during childhood for children with cleft palates or lips, as they may have hearing problems due to ear infections. However, this is not an immediate concern for a newborn.

Orthodontist consultation is usually required before and after an alveolar (gum) bone graft, and lifetime dental care is often necessary. However, this is a long-term consideration rather than an immediate need for the newborn.

After addressing feeding and respiratory issues, further consultations and follow-ups are advised, including general paediatrician, speech pathology, eye review, and chromosomal microarrays for isolated cleft palates. Other specialties may be involved as indicated, such as cardiology and otolaryngology.
learningPoints: Cleft lip and palate is one of the most common congenital craniofacial abnormalities.
The initial evaluation is focused on respiratory and feeding issues to ensure the infant can grow and develop appropriately until the definitive surgical repair.
references: Phalke N, Goldman JJ. Cleft Palate. [Updated 2022 Sep 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK563128/
Hartzell LD, Kilpatrick LA.  Diagnosis and management of patients with clefts: a comprehensive and interdisciplinary approach. Otolaryngol Clin North Am. 2014 Oct; 47(5); 821-52
Gallagher E, McKinney C, Glass R. Promoting Breast Milk Nutrition in Infants with Cleft Lip and Palate. Adv neonatal Care. 2017 Apri;17(2): 79-80
McGuire E. Cleft lip and palates and breastfeeding. Breastfeed Rev 2017; 25:17.
Cleft lip and cleft palate(June 2021).[Internet]. Available from: https://www.healthdirect.gov.au/cleft-lip-and-cleft-palate

South Australian Perinatal Practice Guideline, Cleft Lip and Palate in the Neonatal Period(09/03/2022 ).[Internet]. Available from: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/public+content/sa+health+internet/resources/policies/cleft+lip+and+palate+in+the+neonatal+period+-+sa+perinatal+practice+guidelines
Cleft lip and palate in neonates (16 July, 2014 ).[Internet]. Available from: http://www3.health.vic.gov.au/neonatalhandbook/congenital/cleft-lip-and-palate.htm
